title: Carved beech porridge spurtle
subtitle: Scottish porridge stirrer in green beech, straight and tapered
excerpt: A traditional Scottish porridge spurtle in green beech. Straight tapered rod, blunt tip, simple handle. A beginner project that introduces the push cut on round cross-sections without the complexity of a bowl.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management on Scottish kitchen treen. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on round treen forms.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture. Carves more easily than seasoned wood under hand tools.
A short length of round or split timber, ready to be worked into a blank for carving.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short controlled cut pivoted by the off-hand thumb on the back of the blade. Used for fine shaping where blade-travel is small.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.

content.text: A spurtle is the Scottish porridge stirrer: a straight tapered rod, 30 to 35 cm long, used to keep porridge moving as it cooks so that it does not catch on the pan base. The traditional shape is a thistle head at the top and a blunt taper at the working end. This project uses a simpler form: a straight taper with a small shoulder at the handle end, entirely cut with
push cuts
on the round cross-section. Beech carves cleanly in
green
condition and dries food-safe.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
Billet
: 35 cm long, 4 cm diameter round or 4 by 4 cm square section. Green beech from windfall or coppice off-cut. Ash is a good substitute; sycamore is softer and slightly easier for a first attempt.
Tools
A
sloyd-knife
only; a leather strop; raw linseed oil. No axe needed for a round billet.
Method
If starting from a square billet, take push cuts off the corners along the full length to create a rough octagon in cross-section. Strop after every 10 minutes.
Work the octagon to a circle with
push cuts

along the length. Blade at 30 degrees to the long axis, lifting thin shavings off each flat. After a complete circuit, the cross-section should be 16-sided. Another circuit brings it toward round. Check by rolling the spurtle on a flat surface; a round rod rolls smoothly.
Set the taper: work the working end (the bottom 20 cm) from 3 cm diameter at the mid-shaft down to 1.5 cm at the blunt tip, using push cuts along the taper. The handle end stays full diameter.
Form the shoulder at the handle end with a
stop cut
followed by push cuts from the handle end down to the shoulder. The shoulder is a gentle 5 mm step that gives the hand something to register against during stirring.
Check the round cross-section by rolling on a flat surface and by sighting along the length for any bow. Correct with light push cuts on the high side.
Leave to dry for 10 days on a shelf before finishing.
Finishing
Sand through 120 and 180 grit along the grain. Apply raw linseed oil, let it soak in for one hour, wipe off the surplus. Cure time before food contact is 2 weeks.
Variations
Thistle-head spurtle: carve a small carved thistle or ball at the handle end in place of the simple shoulder. Intermediate level; adds an hour of thumb-pivot carving.
Ash instead of beech: slightly coarser grain but very strong. Good if the billet is from a larger branch.
Shorter stirrer for a small saucepan: reduce the length to 25 cm. Keep the same taper ratio.
Care
Rinse under warm running water after use, towel-dry immediately. The working end goes into hot porridge; the linseed finish holds up well to this. Re-oil monthly when in daily use.

items.symptom: Spurtle bows to one side as it dries
items.cause: Uneven wall thickness on one side of the round cross-section
items.fix: Check the round cross-section before setting aside. Roll the spurtle on the bench; a bow shows as a wobble. Correct with light push cuts on the thick side.

items.cause: Sanding across the grain lifted the fibres on the round cross-section
items.fix: Sand along the grain only. Hold the sandpaper flat to one facet at a time, angled along the long axis.

items.cause: Cutting into rising grain at the shoulder transition
items.fix: Cut from the shoulder downward toward the working end, not from the working end up toward the shoulder.
